coredata.dc:description: Suppression of vascular cell senescence is of great significance in preventing cardiovascular diseases such as hypertension and atherosclerosis. The oxidative stress damage caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS) can lead to cellular senescence. Rapamycin (Rapa) is well known to suppress cell senescence via mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway. However, poor water solubility and lack of ROS scavenging ability limit the further development of Rapa. To improve the solubility of Rapa and endow with ROS scavenging ability, Rapa functionalized carbon dots (Rapa-CDs) are target-oriented synthesized via free radical polymerization combination with hydrothermal carbonization. Rapa-CDs improve the solubility of Rapa and show ROS scavenging abilities. The solubility of Rapa-CDs with 9.41 g is improved 3.6 × 104 times higher than that of Rapa (2.6 × 10-4 g). The half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of Rapa-CDs toward hydroxyl radical (•OH) and 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl free radical (DPPH•) are 0.18 and 0.17 mg/mL, respectively. Rapa-CDs show anti-oxidative stress effect in HEVECs (Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells) via reducing ROS levels by 87 %. Rapa-CDs alleviate HUVECs senescence by suppressing mTOR overactivation, attenuate the expression of P53, P21 and P16. The study demonstrates the target-oriented synthesis of drugs functionalized CDs with anti-senescence via dual-pathway of anti-oxidative stress and mTOR.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapa functionalized carbon dots (Rapa-CDs) were target-oriented synthesized via free radical polymerization combination with hydrothermal carbonization technology. Rapa-CDs has a high synthetic yield of 63%. The solubility of Rapa-CDs with 9.41 g is improved 3.6×104 times higher than that of Rapa (2.6×10-4 g). Rapa-CDs show the ability to scavenge ROS, and suppress cell senescence via anti-oxidative stress and mTOR.

Suppression of vascular cell senescence is of great significance in preventing cardiovascular diseases such as hypertension and atherosclerosis. The oxidative stress damage caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS) can lead to cellular senescence. Rapamycin (Rapa) is well known to suppress cell senescence via mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway. However, poor water solubility and lack of ROS scavenging ability limit the further development of Rapa. To improve the solubility of Rapa and endow with ROS scavenging ability, Rapa functionalized carbon dots (Rapa-CDs) are target-oriented synthesized via free radical polymerization combination with hydrothermal carbonization. Rapa-CDs improve the solubility of Rapa and show ROS scavenging abilities. The solubility of Rapa-CDs with 9.41 g is improved 3.6 × 104 times higher than that of Rapa (2.6 × 10-4 g). The half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of Rapa-CDs toward hydroxyl radical (•OH) and 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl free radical (DPPH•) are 0.18 and 0.17 mg/mL, respectively. Rapa-CDs show anti-oxidative stress effect in HEVECs (Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells) via reducing ROS levels by 87 %. Rapa-CDs alleviate HUVECs senescence by suppressing mTOR overactivation, attenuate the expression of P53, P21 and P16. The study demonstrates the target-oriented synthesis of drugs functionalized CDs with anti-senescence via dual-pathway of anti-oxidative stress and mTOR.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vascular cell senescence represents a pivotal hallmark of vascular senescence and serves as the primary instigator of cardiovascular disorders, including hypertension and atherosclerosis [1,2]. Therefore, the suppression of vascular cell senescence is of great significance for the prevention and treatment of a series of diseases. Two different mechanisms are thought to be the primary contributors of cell senescence. Oxidative stress damage caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS) is one of the mechanisms (ROS concept) [3]. Nutrients and growth factors stimulate mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) signals is the second mechanism (TOR concept) [3–5]. Therefore, the suppression of cellular senescence can be effectively achieved through either anti-oxidative stress mechanisms or the inhibition of mTOR overactivation. Rapamycin (Rapa) can extend the lifespan of multiple model animals and achieve anti-senescence effects by inhibiting mTOR overactivation [6–10]. However, it is almost insoluble in water (only 2.6 × 10-4 g), which seriously hinders its bioavailability [10,11].

To improve its therapeutic effectiveness, the field of nanomedicine has focused on developing a variety of nanodelivery systems, including micelles [12], liposomes [13] and polymers [14]. Rapa-loaded nanoparticles were synthesized by emulsion/evaporation method to improve bioavailability of Rapa [9]. However, the majority of synthesized nanomaterials lack ROS scavenging ability and fundamentally fail to solve the problem of poor solubility of Rapa. Therefore, an anti-aging water-soluble Rapa functional nanomaterial with ROS scavenging ability is urgently needed to solve above problems. Chitosan (CS) derived carbon dots (CDs) have good water solubility, high biocompatibility and ROS scavenging ability [15–19].

Herein, Rapa functionalized CDs (Rapa-CDs) with high solubility and ROS scavenging ability were target-oriented synthesized, which have the abilities to suppress vascular cell senescence. Rapa-CDs with high synthetic yield were target-oriented synthesized via free radical polymerization combination with hydrothermal carbonization by using Rapa, CS and acrylamide (AM) as precursors. Rapa-CDs allow Rapa achieves the transformation from insoluble to soluble in water. Rapa-CDs suppress vascular cell senescence via anti-oxidative stress and mTOR pathways. The half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) values of Rapa-CDs with scavenging ability toward hydroxyl radical (•OH) and 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl free radical (DPPH•) are 0.18 and 0.17 mg/mL, respectively. Rapa-CDs show significant anti-oxidative stress effect in HEVECs (Human Umbilical Vein Endothelial Cells) via reducing ROS levels by 87 %. Additionally, Rapa-CDs alleviate the senescence of HUVECs by suppressing mTOR overactivation, downregulating cellular senescence indicators (SA-β-gal activity, P53, P21 and P16 expression). Taken together, the work proposes a method for the target-oriented synthesis of drugs functionalized CDs, and provided a new strategy for anti-senescence via dual pathways of anti-oxidative stress and mTOR.

CS (deacetylation degree ≥ 95 %), Rapa (≥95 %) and acrylamide (AM, 99.0 %) were purchased from Aladdin. All solutions were prepared using deionized water from a Milli-Q water purification system with resistivity of 18.2 MΩ. Dialysis bags (MWCO = 500 Da) were obtained from Solarbio (USA).

The UV–vis (TU1901) absorbance spectra were measured to evaluate optical properties of CDs. FTIR (Nicolet 380, Thermo Electron Corporation, MA, USA) was used to confirm the functional groups on the surface of CDs. The chemical structure of Rapa moieties was recorded by nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopies (1H NMR, NEXUS-870) using 84 % DMSO and 16 % D2O as solvents. HPLC analysis was carried out on a AcclaimTM 120 instrument. The mobile phase was acetonitrile–water (78:22 v/v). The column temperature was 40 ℃, flow rate was 1.0 mL/min, and the detection wavelength was 277 nm. The surface properties of Rapa-CDs were confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250, Al Kα).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Target-oriented synthesis of Rapa-CDs via free radical polymerization combination with hydrothermal carbonization process
ce:section.ce:para: CS, Rapa and AM were used as precursors to synthesize CDs via free radical polymerization combination with hydrothermal carbonization process. Briefly, 75 mg CS, 50 mg Rapa and 200 mg AM were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water and stirred for 20 min, then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 12 h. After the reaction was naturally cooled to room temperature, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove insoluble impurities. Finally, the CDs was obtained after a dialysis process (500 Da cutoff). The CDs were obtained by changing the amount of CS and Rapa in the precursor, which was used to explore the influence of different contents of CS and Rapa on the performance. The obtained products were labeled as Rapa-CDs. Using the same method, other CDs synthesized by Rapa or CS as the precursor is Rapacont CDs or CScont CDs. See Table S1 for more details.

The synthetic yield of Rapa-CDs was calculated according to equation (1):

Herein, M1 and M0 are the total mass of Rapa-CDs powder and precursor, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapa-CDs standard curve and solubility determination
ce:section.ce:para: Took appropriate amount of Rapa-CDs, added deionized water to prepare 0.003, 0.005, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5 mg/mL Rapa-CDs solution. The absorbance was measured at a certain wavelength, and each position was measured three times in parallel. The standard curve of Rapa-CDs was obtained by linear regression based on absorbance. Then, Rapa-CDs was prepared into a saturated aqueous solution, which was fully dissolved by ultrasound for 30 min, and then centrifugated at 12,000 r/min for 10 min. Diluted the saturated solution 200 times and took 3 mL supernatant to determine the absorbance. The solubility of Rapa-CDs in water was obtained by calculating the concentration according to the standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Suppress vascular cell senescence via anti-oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para:
•OH-scavenging activity: The •OH scavenging activity was determined according to the reported method [20]. The Fenton reaction was used to form •OH, which react with salicylic acid to form 2, 3-dihydroxybenzoic acid. This substance has a special absorption at 510 nm. The •OH scavenging ability of Rapa-CDs was quantitatively evaluated by observing the decrease of the specific absorption at 510 nm. FeSO4 (9 mmol/L), salicylic acid (9 mmol/L) and Rapa-CDs with different concentrations were mixed. Rapa-CDs solution was replaced by deionized water in the control group. Then, H2O2 (8.8 mmol/L) was added to the solution. The •OH scavenging activity of Rapa-CDs was calculated by the following equation:
(2)
OHscavengingactivity =
A 0
-
A x
/
A 0
100 %
Here, A0 and Ax are the absorbance at 510 nm of the control group and the experimental group, respectively.

DPPH•-scavenging activity: DPPH• is a kind of stable free radical. When radical scavenger is added to the solution of DPPH•, the lone pair electrons are paired, and the dark purple DPPH• is reduced to form the yellow DPPH-H non-free radical. DPPH• has the maximum absorption peak near 517 nm. Therefore, the ethanol solution of DPPH• was mixed with Rapa-CDs solution of different concentrations (deionized water was the control group), and the absorbance value of the solution at 517 nm was measured to quantitatively evaluate the scavenging ability of Rapa-CDs on DPPH•. The calculation formula is as follows [21,22]:
(3)

Wherein, H0 and Hx are the absorbance at 517 nm of the control and Rapa-CDs, respectively.

Cell culture and treatment: HUVECs were cultured in DMEM/F12 medium (GIBCO) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (Excell) and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin at 37 ℃ in 5 % CO2. To induce senescence, HUVECs were treated with 100 µM H2O2 for 1 h and then cultured for another 24 h at 37 ℃. During the treatment, the intervention group was co-cultured with either Rapa or Rapa-CDs.

Construction of anti-oxidative stress model: H2O2 medium (200 µM) was added to HUVECs and cultured for 6 h to induce oxidative stress. Rapa (200 nM) or Rapa/CS-CDs (182.83 ng/mL) were added to the cells in the treatment group during the whole process of induced aging. DCFH-DA probe (Beyotime) detected ROS production, and Malondialdehyde (MDA, Beyotime) assessed oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Suppress vascular cell senescence via mTOR
ce:section.ce:para: Senescence-Associated β-Galactosidase Activity: The senescence properties of Rapa-CDs were evaluated by β-galactosidase staining (Beyotime). Cells were washed in PBS and then fixed at room temperature with fixation fluid for 15 min. After washing three times, cells were incubated overnight with dyeing liquor at 37 ℃. At the end of the incubation, five random fields were counted per dish to assess the percentage of senescence-associated beta-galactosidase (SA-β-gal) positive cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Target-oriented synthesis of Rapa-CDs for suppression of vascular cell senescence
ce:section.ce:para: Target-oriented conversion of precursors was achieved by free radical polymerization combination with hydrothermal carbonization, Rapa-CDs with high solubility and ROS scavenging ability were obtained (Scheme 1 ). AM monomers were polymerized to form polyacrylamide (PAM), and PAM chains transferred to CS and Rapa. Then PAM with Rapa and CS chains were cross-linked, followed by hydrothermal carbonization to obtain Rapa-CDs. Rapa-CDs not only have a high synthetic yield of 63 %, but also are soluble in water with a solubility of 9.41 g. Rapa-CDs suppress vascular cell senescence via anti-oxidative stress and mTOR pathways. Rapa-CDs have ROS scavenging ability and show significant anti-oxidative stress effect in HEVECs. Rapa-CDs inhibit HUVECs senescence by suppressing mTOR overactivation, downregulating cellular senescence indicators (SA-β-gal activity, P53, P21 and P16 expression).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Target-oriented synthesis of Rapa-CDs via free radical polymerization combination with hydrothermal carbonization
ce:section.ce:para:
Rapa-CDs were characterized by FTIR to identify the chemical modifications of CS and Rapa on Rapa-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1
a, the broad peak at 2810–3022 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CH [23–25]. In the FTIR spectra of Rapa and Rapa-CDs, the peaks at 1715 and 1632 cm−1 correspond to ketone (CO) and CC of Rapa, respectively [26–28]. The peak at 978 cm−1 of Rapa is assigned as the out of plane deformation of CC. Meanwhile, these characteristic peaks of CS-CDs and Rapa-CDs at 1560, 1382 and 834 cm−1 are attributed to NH, CN and β-1,4-glycosidic bonds, respectively [29,30]. These results imply Rapa and CS are modified on Rapa-CDs. 1H NMR spectrum further demonstrates the chemical structure of Rapa-CDs (Fig. 1 b). The 1H NMR spectrum of Rapa-CDs containing peaks at 0.69, 1.28, 1.37, 1.52 and 7.68 ppm indicate the existence of H2, H4, H5, H6 and H11 in Rapa [31] (Fig. 1 c). The peaks at 2.01–1.93 ppm is due to glucosamine residue (H7-H10) of CS [32–34] (Fig. 1 c). Two peaks at 0.64 and 0.95 ppm are corresponding to H1 and H3 in PAM [35] (Fig. 1 c). The 1H NMR results indicate the presence of Rapa and CS moieties, and the mass content of Rapa is about 1.4 wt%. Rapa and CS moieties were successfully grafted to CDs. Although the conversion rate of Rapa is lower, it is still possible to achieve the same anti-senescence effect as Rapa.

Rapa, CS-CDs and Rapa-CDs were characterized by reversed-phase HPLC to determine the presence of Rapa and CS moieties (Fig. 1 d). The chromatogram of Rapa possesses one main peak in retention time of 10 min. Rapa-CDs has a weak peak in 11 min in comparison to CS-CDs, indicating the presence of Rapa moieties [36]. The hydroxyl group on Rapa were replaced by ether, the polarity became smaller, and the retention time became longer. There was a peak at 2.35 min, which proved Rapa was successfully graft. Rapa-CDs and CS-CDs had peaks at 2.06 and 2.28 min, which could be attributed to PAM and PAM-CS [37,38]. The results indicated CS and Rapa modified Rapa-CDs were successfully prepared, and the structure diagram is shown in Fig. 1 e. Additionally, XPS results show that Rapa-CDs are composed of C, N and O ( Fig. S1 ). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 1 f) show three peaks at 284.7, 285.6, and 288.1 eV, which were assigned to CC, CN, and CO, respectively [39,40]. In N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1 g), the two peaks at 399.6 and 401.2 eV ascribed to pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, respectively [20]. In O 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 1 h), two peaks at 532.3 and 531.3 eV represented CO and CO bonds, respectively [41].

To understand the high-synthetic yield Rapa-CDs (63 %) with ROS scavenging and anti-senescence ability in detail, the target-oriented synthesis mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 2 . In the early stages of hydrothermal, the CC of AM form an initial free radical, followed by solution polymerization to form PAM fragment with active free radical in the hydrothermal conditions [42]. Subsequently, PAM fragment with active free radical chain transfer to CS or Rapa in the solvent (Scheme 2 a) [43,44]. PAM and CS or Rapa are linked by covalent bonds to form PAM-CS or PAM-Rapa [45]. Then, PAM-CS and PAM-Rapa are crosslinked and dehydrated to form PAM-CS/PAM-Rapa clusters (Scheme 2 b) [46]. Finally, the generated PAM-CS/PAM-Rapa clusters undergo hydrothermal carbonization to form Rapa-CDs (Scheme 2 c). When Rapa or CS is used as the precursor to prepare Rapacont CDs or CScont CDs, the synthetic yield is as low as 5.7 % or 11.8 %. The addition of AM in the precursor improved the synthetic yield to 63 %, an increase of nearly 11 or 5 times, respectively (Scheme 2 d). This is because the CC polymerization in AM can avoid side reactions and thus improve the synthetic yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapa-CDs realize Rapa transformation from insoluble to soluble in water
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–Vis absorption of Rapa-CDs is shown in Fig. S2 . The CS-CDs and Rapa-CDs show optical absorption in 317 nm attributed to the n-π* transition of CO bands [47]. Notably, Rapa-CDs has an absorption peak at 277 nm, which corresponds to the characteristic absorption peak of Rapa [48,49], proving the presence of Rapa in Rapa-CDs. The poor water solubility of Rapa (2.6 × 10-4 g) [11] limits its application. However, the water solubility can be greatly improved by using Rapa as a precursor to synthesize CDs. Rapa-CDs have good water solubility at concentration of 0.5–20 mg/mL (Fig. 2
a). The absorbance values of Rapa-CDs with different concentrations at the maximum absorption wavelength (317 nm) were measured. The solubility of Rapa-CDs is 9.41 g according to the calculation based on linear equation (Fig. 2 b), which is about 3.6 × 104 larger times than that of Rapa (2.6 × 10-4 g). Further, TEM images display Rapa-CDs are spherical and evenly dispersed with an average particle size of 5.7 nm (Fig. 2 c and d) [50,51].

Fluorescence spectra of Rapa-CDs show the FL emission peak shifts to a longer wavelengthfrequency as the excitation wavelength increases from 360 nm to 450 nm (Fig. 2 e). These excitation-dependent properties are characteristic of Rapa-CDs behavior. When Rapa-CDs was excited at 400 nm, the maximum emission wavelength was observed at 488 nm, which is also well supported by 3D contour plot of Rapa-CDs (Fig. 2 f). Further, the internalization of Rapa-CDs by HUVECs were investigated. Confocal imaging revealed the uptake of Rapa-CDs by HUVECs, as indicated by the green fluorescence signal. The confocal image of HUVECs with green color (Fig. 2 g–i) indicated Rapa-CDs can be uploaded by HUVECs, while the cells without Rapa-CDs-treated cells do not have fluorescence, demonstrating Rapa-CDs-treated cells can be internalized by HUVECs [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapa-CDs for suppression of vascular cell senescence via anti-oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para:
•OH and DPPH• are typically used as indicators to assess ROS scavenging activity of Rapa-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3
a, Rapa-CDs can react with •OH and DPPH• to generate H2O and DPPH-H, respectively, which reflects the scavenging ability of ROS. According to Formula 2, the •OH elimination efficiency of Rapa-CDs with different concentrations were calculated (Fig. 3 b and c). All the Rapa-CDs showed certain scavenging ability toward •OH. The scavenging efficiency of •OH was concentration-dependent with the increase of Rapa-CDs concentration. Moreover, when the concentration of Rapa-CDs (CS: 25, 50, 75 mg) was 0.30 mg/mL, the •OH elimination efficiency was 52.5, 74.5 and 84.8 %, respectively. However, with the increase of Rapa content in the precursor, the •OH elimination efficiency did not change significantly. Similarly, when the concentration of Rapa-CDs (Rapa: 0, 25, 50 mg) was 0.30 mg/mL, the •OH elimination efficiency are 81.8, 82.5 and 84.8 %, respectively. Moreover, the IC50 value of Rapa-CDs (CS: Rapa = 75:50 mg) toward •OH is the lowest, which is 0.18 mg/mL (Fig. 3 d). These results indicate the •OH scavenging ability of Rapa-CDs can be attributable to the ability of CS. In addition, the scavenging activity of Rapa-CDs toward DPPH• was also determined. As shown in Fig. 3 e and f, Rapa-CDs can effectively eliminate DPPH•. As the contents of CS and Rapa in the precursor increased, the scavenging ability was improved, and IC50 of Rapa-CDs is 0.17 mg/mL (Fig. 3 g). These results indicated Rapa-CDs (CS: Rapa = 75:50 mg) have a good ROS scavenging activity toward •OH and DPPH•. In order to clarify the scavenging ability of Rapa-CDs on ROS, some published works were compared with this work, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 h. Rapa-CDs possess excellent •OH [20,53–57] and DPPH• scavenging ability [20,53,54,56–59].

There are two pathways cause cell senescence, ROS concept and TOR concept. Therefore, the anti-senescence properties of Rapa-CDs were explored through the dual perspectives of mitigating oxidative stress and modulating mTOR pathways. The oxidative stress model was constructed to explore the ROS scavenging effect of Rapa-CDs in HUVECs, using H2O2 induces intracellular ROS levels. The cells were exposed to Rapa and Rapa-CDs, without H2O2 triggered HUVECs as the control, and monitored by a ROS DCFH-DA probe, which reacts with ROS producing green fluorescence. Compared with the control group, the green fluorescence intensity of the H2O2-induced group was significantly increased, indicating intracellular ROS was up-regulated after H2O2 stimulation (Fig. 4
a).

However, the fluorescence intensity of both the Rapa and Rapa-CDs treatment groups exhibited a notable decrease. Particularly, the reduction was more pronounced in the Rapa-CDs group. The results proved Rapa-CDs have obvious scavenging effect on ROS. Moreover, the bar chart was used to further analyze the ROS levels in each cell (Fig. 4 b). The ROS level in HUVECs stimulated by H2O2 were significantly higher than that of control group. Following treatment with Rapa or Rapa-CDs, the ROS level was decreased compared to the H2O2 treatment group. Notably, the reduction rate of ROS levels in the Rapa-CDs group was approximately 87 %, indicating a significant scavenging effect of Rapa-CDs on HUVECs. Meanwhile, MDA was used to evaluate oxidative stress. As displayed in Fig. 4 c, compared with the H2O2 treatment group, the Rapa-CDs group has a significant anti-oxidative stress effect. Many studies have shown the presence of excessive ROS causes oxidative stress, which can lead to cell damage and death, resulting in accelerated cell senescence [60–62]. Based on the above oxidative stress model experimental results, it is found Rapa-CDs have ROS scavenging effect and anti-oxidative stress ability. Therefore, CS-derived Rapa-CDs possess the capability of ROS scavenging and mitigating oxidative stress, thereby potentially suppressing vascular cell senescence (Fig. 4 d) [15,63,64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapa-CDs for suppression of vascular cell senescence via mTOR pathway
ce:section.ce:para:
SA-β-gal activity were evaluated to identify the effect of Rapa-CDs on H2O2-induced senescence in cultured HUVECs. Observed the expression of P53, P21 and P16, which are characteristic indicators of cellular senescence [65]. As exhibited in Fig. 5
a and b, the senescence model of HUVECs was established by exposing them to 100 µM H2O2, resulting in a significant increase in SA-β-gal activity. While after the treatment of the same mass of Rapa and Rapa-CDs, the degree of senescence is significantly reduced. The results indicated Rapa-CDs had the similar anti-senescence function as the Rapa. Moreover, Rapa-CDs exhibited significant suppression of mRNA expression levels of senescence-associated genes P53, P21, and P16 (Fig. 5 c–e), which further reveals the suppressing vascular cell senescence effect of Rapa-CDs. According to the theory of mTOR affect senescence, growth factors and nutrients activate mTOR, suppress autophagy and promote protein synthesis. When mTOR is overactivated, resulting in cell damage accumulation, increased SA-β-gal activity and expression of P53, P21 and P16, leading to vascular cell senescence (Fig. 5 f) [66,67]. Rapa can suppress mTOR signaling pathway, reduce cell damage and suppress vascular cell senescence [68,69]. Therefore, Rapa-CDs can suppress vascular cell senescence via mTOR as same as Rapa. Since the content of Rapa in Rapa-CDs is about 1.4 wt%, the same anti-senescence effect of Rapa-CDs and Rapa is partly caused by anti-oxidative stress ability of Rapa-CDs [60].

In summary, Rapa-CDs with high solubility and dual-pathway suppression of vascular cell senescence function have been target-oriented synthesized via free radical polymerization combination with hydrothermal carbonization. Rapa-CDs improved the solubility of Rapa and endowed it with ROS scavenging ability. The solubility of Rapa-CDs with 9.41 g was significantly improved as higher as 3.6 × 104 times than that of Rapa (2.6 × 10-4 g). Compared with Rapa, Rapa-CDs possessed ROS scavenging ability, IC50 values toward •OH and DPPH• were 0.18 and 0.17 mg/mL, respectively. Rapa-CDs showed significant anti-oxidative stress effect in HEVECs via reducing ROS levels by 87 %. Rapa-CDs could effectively suppress vascular cell senescence via anti-oxidative stress and mTOR pathways. This work provides a promising way for suppression of cell senescence based on dual pathways of down-regulating oxidative stress and specific signal pathways of drug, and further exploration of its potential in clinical diagnostics will be an important part of future research.
